title: U.S.-China Relations Are More Volatile Than Ever
gist: Despite Trump and Xi's May 2026 summit pledge of 'constructive strategic stability,' both leaders have purged institutions and personnel responsible for managing crises, so U.S.-China relations are now more unpredictable and dangerous than at any prior point, with the core problem framed as institutional rather than geopolitical.

insights.claim: At their May 2026 Beijing summit, Trump and Xi agreed to promote a 'constructive relationship of strategic stability,' a deliberately vague phrase the two sides likely interpret differently, signaling shared aspiration but not real convergence on managing U.S.-China differences.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: Do not read the May 2026 summit's 'strategic stability' language as a durable de-escalation commitment; it is ambiguous phrasing papering over unresolved structural risk.
insights.locator: At their May summit in Beijing

insights.claim: The authors' central thesis is that despite positive top-level summit sentiment, U.S.-China cooperation is now more challenging and precarious than ever because both Trump and Xi have centralized power and damaged the institutions, communication channels, and bureaucracies that previously prevented crises from escalating to the brink.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: Treat U.S.-China relations as structurally more crisis-prone than headline diplomacy suggests; the risk of miscalculation-driven escalation (e.g., over Taiwan) is elevated regardless of top-level statements of goodwill.
insights.locator: Yet neither strategic trust nor friendship is sufficient for stability

themes.theme: Parallel purges of expertise on both sides

insights.claim: In the U.S., the Department of Government Efficiency slashed spending and terminated government employees en masse, while dozens of generals were demoted or denied promotion and hundreds of diplomats were fired or pushed into early retirement, framed by Trump as targeting 'woke' bureaucrats and waste.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: U.S. foreign-policy and military institutional capacity has been materially degraded through personnel purges, reducing the bureaucratic bench strength available to manage a fast-moving China crisis.
insights.locator: In Washington, the so-called Department of Government Efficiency

insights.claim: In China, relentless anti-corruption campaigns have become the primary mechanism for removing officials seen as disloyal to Xi personally; of officers purged across the Chinese military through February 2026, 61 percent held key operational roles, and the foreign-policy bureaucracy has been hit hard, including the 2023 disappearance of Foreign Minister Qin Gang (the shortest-serving PRC foreign minister in history) and the 2025 detention of Liu Jianchao, one of his likely successors.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: China's purge of operational military and diplomatic officials (61% of purged officers in key operational roles) signals real degradation of institutional competence and continuity in China's foreign and defense policymaking apparatus, not just political theater.
insights.locator: Of those officers purged across the Chinese military through February

insights.claim: China's Central Military Commission, the supreme military leadership body meant to advise the commander in chief, has been slashed by Xi's purges of top generals down to just two members — Xi himself as civilian chair and Zhang Shengmin, head of the CMC's Discipline Inspection Commission — leaving it unable to fulfill its professional advisory function.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: China's top military decision-making body is now effectively hollowed out to two people, one of whom is a discipline/loyalty enforcer rather than an operational commander — a significant governance risk factor for how Beijing would handle a fast-moving military crisis.
insights.locator: In China, Xi's ongoing purges of top military leaders

insights.claim: In the U.S., Secretary of State Marco Rubio also serves as national security advisor but appears focused on his State Department portfolio and distracted by his role as chief administrator for Venezuela, while his changes to National Security Council processes have rendered the NSC largely defective as an interagency coordination body.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: The NSC's degraded interagency coordination function under a dual-hatted, overstretched Rubio is a specific institutional weak point in U.S. crisis-management capacity toward China.
insights.locator: However, Secretary of State Marco Rubio

themes.theme: Loss of clear counterparts on both sides

insights.claim: Neither Washington nor Beijing can clearly identify who, besides Trump and Xi themselves, is authoritatively empowered to negotiate on a given issue: Chinese Defense Minister Dong Jun has never been appointed to the CMC despite holding the post since 2023 (a break from precedent), making his role largely honorific, while on the U.S. side it is unclear whether Hegseth, Rubio, Lutnick, Bessent, or Jared Kushner owns China trade and security policy.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: The absence of clear counterpart authority on both sides raises the probability of miscommunication or missed signals during a fast-developing crisis, since normal channel-based crisis diplomacy assumes identifiable empowered negotiators.
insights.locator: The first is the erosion of clarity

themes.theme: Taiwan as the highest-risk flashpoint under institutional dysfunction

insights.claim: Trump has called a $14 billion U.S. arms package to Taiwan (currently on hold despite congressional pre-approval) 'a very good negotiating chip,' suggesting willingness to trade Taiwan policy for other gains, even as the administration continues alliance-like training and weapons support to Taiwan's armed forces — a contradictory signal set.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: Watch the $14 billion Taiwan arms package's on-hold status as a live indicator of whether Trump is actually willing to trade Taiwan commitments for other US-China deals — a potentially destabilizing signal for Taiwan-exposed assets if the package is cancelled or renegotiated.
insights.locator: On the one hand, Trump's recent public comments

insights.claim: Beijing views successive U.S. administrations as having failed to honor 1970s/early-1980s commitments to walk away from Taiwan, and the authors argue Beijing itself would be unlikely to honor any new deal to reduce pressure on Taipei, citing that after Nancy Pelosi's 2022 Taiwan visit China shifted permanently into 'high gear' with heavy gray-zone pressure tactics against Taiwan's DPP-led governments that a new deal is unlikely to reverse.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: Skepticism warranted on any near-term U.S.-China Taiwan 'grand bargain' actually reducing Chinese military pressure on Taiwan durably — precedent since 2022 suggests Beijing's gray-zone posture is now a fixed baseline, not a negotiable variable.
insights.locator: Since then, China has operated in one mode vis-à-vis Taiwan

themes.theme: The real risk is institutional, not ideological

insights.claim: The authors argue the danger is not a new Cold War but a highly unstable form of great-power competition in which both sides are powerful, insecure, and increasingly unpredictable, because concentration of personal power around Trump and Xi weakens the institutions that make even stable competition possible.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: Reframe U.S.-China tail risk away from 'ideological Cold War conflict' toward 'accidental crisis mismanagement due to hollowed-out institutions' — the latter is arguably harder to hedge because it depends on unpredictable individual decision-making rather than legible policy trajectories.
insights.quote: The danger, then, is not a new cold war but a highly unstable form of great-power competition in which both sides are powerful, insecure, and increasingly unpredictable.
insights.locator: The danger, then, is not a new cold war

insights.claim: The authors conclude that a stable U.S.-China relationship does not require friendship, strategic trust, or resolution of all major disputes, but does require functioning institutions, reliable communication channels, and adequate bureaucratic competence — conditions currently absent on both sides.
insights.speaker: Andrew Scobell, Andrea Ghiselli
insights.so_what: Use institutional-capacity indicators (NSC functioning, CMC staffing, identifiable counterparts) rather than rhetorical warmth between Trump and Xi as the leading indicator of real U.S.-China crisis-management risk.
insights.locator: A stable U.S.-China relationship does not require friendship
